articles.title: Understanding Server-Side Rendering in Angular
articles.excerpt: Learn how Angular renders HTML on the server before sending it to the browser.
articles.author: Demo Team
articles.body:
SSR (Server-Side Rendering) means your Angular app runs on Node during the initial request. The server executes components, resolves data, and returns fully-formed HTML.

Crawlers and users see meaningful content immediately—no empty <app-root> shell.

In this demo, look at View Page Source on any route: article titles and product names appear in the raw HTML because they were rendered on the server.

articles.title: Meta Tags, Open Graph, and Twitter Cards
articles.excerpt: Why crawlers and social platforms need rich metadata on every page.
articles.author: Demo Team
articles.body:
SEO is how you help search engines understand your pages. Dynamic <title>, meta descriptions, canonical URLs, and JSON-LD structured data all signal relevance.

Open Graph and Twitter Card tags control how links look when shared on Slack, LinkedIn, or X.

articles.title: TransferState: Avoiding Duplicate API Calls
articles.excerpt: SSR fetches data on the server—without TransferState the browser fetches again.
articles.author: Demo Team
articles.body:
When SSR and the browser both call the same endpoint, you pay twice: slower hydration and wasted bandwidth.

TransferState serializes server-fetched data into the HTML; the client reuses it instead of refetching.

Visit the Transfer State Demo page and open the Network tab to compare both patterns.
